posts.cooked:
Birthday is coming up in a month and have always wanted to do the whole birth-year wine thing (cheezy but, hey it’s fun). We’re having a dinner at our house and I wanted to get a few bottles from 1978 that are more likely than not to be alive and well. I know that I’m most likely looking at old-world Bordeaux types which is great as that is right in my wheelhouse.

So any ideas? Also if anyone is holding any extra '78s and would be willing to part with them I’d live to work a trade out. Thanks, T

posts.name: T. Schaffler

TSimpson:

Birthday is coming up in a month and have always wanted to do the whole birth-year wine thing (cheezy but, hey it’s fun). We’re having a dinner at our house and I wanted to get a few bottles from 1978 that are more likely than not to be alive and well. I know that I’m most likely looking at old-world Bordeaux types which is great as that is right in my wheelhouse.

So any ideas? Also if anyone is holding any extra '78s and would be willing to part with them I’d live to work a trade out. Thanks, T

T,

I too have started to try and find 78’s in good condition to open up on my 40th in an unfortunate 3 years, 1.5 months .

That being said, I was able to find some 1978 Nicolas Potel’s, Collection Bellenum which are for the most part in pristine condition because they have only been in bottle for about 6 years (All vintages were bottled in 2009 directly from barrel). I picked up 2 bottles the other day, and they should have about 8 bottles left. See the link below.

Also, I’ve had multiple bottles of different vintages, ranging from the early 60’s to early 70’s and for the most part, they have been pretty good. I also have a 1969 Potel, Volnay Santenots lying around as well.

Hope this helps!

http://www.finewinesinternational.com/do/product/1978_Roche_de_Bellene_Pommard_Les_Pezerolles_1er_cru_25446

posts.name: Dany_K h l o t h
posts.cooked:
T,

I have not opened any of the 78’s yet. I actually haven’t even picked them up yet. Lol.

However, I have tried a 196x (Can’t remember the exact year, but it was an early 60’s vintage) and a 1972, both at a friend’s dinner a couple of years ago and they were both pretty stellar and I’ve been looking for them ever since (at auctions and via ws whenever possible).

I’m swinging by the shop tomorrow to pick them up on my way home from work and was planning on opening one up this July for my birthday.

If it has held up well and I think it will make it to 2018 (which it should); I may buy a couple more, just so I can have a few bottles for my 40th.

Also, if you want, I can email you a picture of the bottle condition. I had merchant email me a picture of all of the bottles before I decided to buy them, and they all seem to be in mint condition.

You can pm me with your email and I’ll send them to you.

-Dany

posts.name: Steve Nellessen

posts.name: Thomas Keim
posts.cooked:
With prices all over the place on the '78s - it can be a crap shoot - especially on high end Bordeaux and Burgundy - But there are bargains to be found. I am seeing the '78 Pichon Lalande listing for under $200 a bottle - '78 Palmer for $200 - $250 - and last year I picked up 6 bottles of 1978 d’Angludet for $25.00 a bottle -

But, it really is a great vintage everywhere (but Germany) and worth taking a few chances -

And I don’t know of one California winery that made a bad Cabernet in 1978 - and I think the bulk of them (if stored right) should be in fine shape - even the little ones.

And don’t be afraid to take a chance on Petite Chateaux/Cru Bourgeois from '78 as well -

posts.name: L e o F r o k i c
posts.cooked: Another vote for Cali Cab, they are great wines across the board.

posts.name: L e o F r o k i c
posts.cooked: Also, I would look for a good Barolo, Piemonte did very well in 1978.
posts.display_username: L e o F r o k i c

posts.name: Cole Kendall
posts.cooked: Domaine de Chevalier is very good as is the Pichon Lalande noted above. My 78 Margaux have not shown that well in recent years.

posts.name: Jay T.
posts.cooked:
L e o F r o k i c:

Also, I would look for a good Barolo, Piemonte did very well in 1978.

I think this really is your best bet if you can find some good bottles. My wife was born in 1978. We’re obviously relative youngsters and have not had a lot of 1978 (although a magnum of 1978 Mondavi Reserve was fantastic 3 years ago), but we’ve had about 10 bottles of 1978 Barolo in the past 4 years, and every single one of them has been fantastic. We’ve probably been unusually lucky with bottle condition/variation issues, but in terms of aging and longevity, I doubt there is a 1978 wine from anywhere else that is in a better sweetspot right now than Barolo.

A few recommendations that you should be able to find for under $300 if you look around for awhile: Cavallotto Bricco Boschis Riserva Vigna San Giuseppe, Marcarini Brunate, G. Mascarello Villero (may have crept up since I bought 2 years ago).

NOTE: after typing out the above, I looked around on Wine-Searcher and was shocked to find that there is hardly any 1978 Barolo for sale in the United States right now. This is a huge change from 2 years ago, when there were hundreds of bottles available online. Prices seem to have gone up a lot too. I suspect that the hype around the 2010 Barolo vintage has had a significant affect on the Barolo market in general in the United States, unfortunately. It looks like it’s going to be increasingly hard to backfill in the near future. Very sad. That said, I’d still keep a look-out, especially at Chambers Street and at online auctions.

If you end up with some 1978’s from California, try to get solid information re: provenance and storage. A lot of the wines back then weren’t stored in the kind of temperature & humidity controlled conditions one might expect now.

Bruce

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
NED VALOIS:

1978 Chateauneuf du Pape Chateau Rayas, 154NY 5/21/2015 $ 800.00 AUCTION:Bottl

And one will never know (or only after opening, if you know the ´78 before) if it was genuine or not!
Remember that the corks were not vintage branded before 1996, and the only identification of the vintage is the triangle label … which can easily be exchanged.

I would never buy a top-Rayas-vintage before 1996 without knowing the exact proveniance of the bottle …

(a friend of mine once bought a “1990” Rayas for a lot of money and served it to me … from the appearance and taste it must have been a 1992 or - more probably - a 1993 )

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked: '78 Mondavi Reserve in large format.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked:
Barolo: 1978 was not just a very good year in Barolo. It was a monster year, with many Barolo-obsessives agreeing that it is either the best year in Barolo of our lifetimes, or one of the top 2 or 3. Prices from that vintage have gone nuts in the last few years. so this is both a fantastic option and an unlikely one unless money is no object.

Bordeaux is a really good choice. I’ve enjoyed several of the '78 Cheval Blanc in the last few years and it has been excellent. Soft, mellow, smokey, rich, definitely a well-aged wine but still utterly enjoyable.

posts.name: George Chadwick
posts.cooked: Last night had a 1978 Matanzas Creek Merlot with a totally red soaked spongy cork. Good! So if you like older cab I’d do that. That 78 Shafer is awesome but super expensive.

I’m a '78 and I’ll pile on for the 1978 Mondavi Reserve, have had a few over the years and they’ve always been good to great. A 1978 Stag’s Leap Cask 23 showed nicely in 2009, as did a 1978 William Hill Reserve, which could be a good “value” option if you can find one. That said, the best bottle I’ve had was an immaculate bottle of 1978 Heitz Fay Vineyard I picked up from Envoyer a few years ago that had perfect provenance. What an awesome wine that was.

posts.name: Christopher P Lavin
posts.cooked: Justino’s and Broadbent Madeira

posts.name: Ken Vastola
posts.cooked: So many good and great wines in 1978. Barolo, Barbaresco, but also Alto Piemonte (e.g. Vallana Spanna) which is much less costly. As has already been mentioned, Diamond Creek (though my favorite is the Volcanic Hill), Montelena. Really all the top cabs. Rhone, Burgundy, in Bordeaux, I think it was good rather than great.

I honestly have my doubts about the 1990 La Chapelle, and sold the bottles I had. After getting half way through a case I concluded the wine was overripe and getting more pruney and oxidative with each bottle. To my mind the last great La Chapelle is actually the '88.

Back on topic, the '78 Jaboulet Cornas was absolutely fantastic at the end of 2014! One of the very greatest Cornas I’ve ever tasted. So that is well worth checking out if you ever see it!
